content.text: Consent Audit Trail and Regulatory Reporting

Regulators auditing GDPR compliance or investigating a breach will ask one question: "Show me exactly when each user consented, to what, under which policy version, and when they withdrew." Without a tamper-evident audit trail, that question cannot be answered reliably. This sub-subtopic covers how to build an append-only, hash-chained audit log for consent events, and how to generate the regulatory reports — DSARs, DPO summaries, and breach evidence packages — that compliance teams and data protection authorities expect.

attrs.alt: Consent audit trail and reporting pipeline

content.text: What the Audit Trail Must Capture
User ID or org ID — never raw name/email in the log
Version of privacy policy the user accepted
How consent was captured (checkbox, API, ToS section)
Which application surface captured the consent
SHA-256 of IP; not raw IP to avoid PII in audit log
Hash of the previous log entry for chain integrity
SHA-256 of this entry's content (excluding this field)

Each audit entry includes the SHA-256 hash of the previous entry. Any deletion or modification breaks the chain and is detectable by a verification pass. This is the same technique used in certificate transparency logs.

from __future__ import annotations
import hashlib
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

GENESIS_HASH = "0" * 64   # sentinel for first entry

@dataclass
class AuditEntry:
    sequence: int
    subject_id: str
    purpose: str
    granted: bool
    captured_at: str              # ISO 8601
    policy_version: str
    mechanism: str
    app_id: str
    ip_hash: Optional[str]
    user_agent_hash: Optional[str]
    withdrawal_reason: Optional[str]
    prev_entry_hash: str
    entry_hash: str = ""          # computed after creation

    def compute_hash(self) -> str:
        """SHA-256 of all fields except entry_hash itself."""
        payload = {k: v for k, v in asdict(self).items() if k != "entry_hash"}
        serialised = json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=True)
        return hashlib.sha256(serialised.encode()).hexdigest()

class ConsentAuditLog:
    """Append-only hash-chained audit log for consent events."""

    def __init__(self, log_path: Path) -> None:
        self._path = log_path
        self._last_hash: str = GENESIS_HASH
        self._sequence: int = 0
        self._load_tail()

    def _load_tail(self) -> None:
        """Read the last entry to restore chain state on restart."""
        if not self._path.exists():
            return
        lines = [l for l in self._path.read_text().splitlines() if l.strip()]
        if not lines:
            return
        last = json.loads(lines[-1])
        self._last_hash = last["entry_hash"]
        self._sequence = last["sequence"] + 1

    def append(self, record) -> AuditEntry:
        """Append a consent record to the chain."""
        entry = AuditEntry(
            sequence=self._sequence,
            subject_id=record.subject_id,
            purpose=record.purpose.value,
            granted=record.granted,
            captured_at=record.captured_at.isoformat(),
            policy_version=record.policy_version,
            mechanism=record.mechanism.value,
            app_id=record.app_id,
            ip_hash=record.ip_hash,
            user_agent_hash=record.user_agent_hash,
            withdrawal_reason=record.withdrawal_reason,
            prev_entry_hash=self._last_hash,
        )
        entry.entry_hash = entry.compute_hash()
        with self._path.open("a") as fh:
            fh.write(json.dumps(asdict(entry)) + "\n")
        self._last_hash = entry.entry_hash
        self._sequence += 1
        return entry

    def verify_chain(self) -> dict:
        """Walk the entire log and verify every link. Returns a tamper report."""
        if not self._path.exists():
            return {"status": "empty", "entries": 0}

        entries = [json.loads(l) for l in self._path.read_text().splitlines() if l.strip()]
        errors = []
        expected_prev = GENESIS_HASH

        for idx, raw in enumerate(entries):
            if raw["sequence"] != idx:
                errors.append(f"Sequence gap at position {idx}: expected {idx}, got {raw['sequence']}")
            if raw["prev_entry_hash"] != expected_prev:
                errors.append(f"Hash mismatch at sequence {raw['sequence']}: "
                               f"expected prev={expected_prev[:12]}..., got {raw['prev_entry_hash'][:12]}...")
            # Re-compute this entry's hash to detect field tampering
            claimed = raw.pop("entry_hash")
            recomputed = hashlib.sha256(
                json.dumps(raw, sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=True).encode()
            ).hexdigest()
            raw["entry_hash"] = claimed
            if claimed != recomputed:
                errors.append(f"Entry {raw['sequence']} content tampered: claimed={claimed[:12]}..., "
                               f"recomputed={recomputed[:12]}...")
            expected_prev = claimed

        return {
            "status": "intact" if not errors else "tampered",
            "entries": len(entries),
            "errors": errors,
        }

Under GDPR Article 15, a data subject may request a copy of all personal data held about them, including the complete consent history. The DSAR report must be produced within 30 days and must include every grant, withdrawal, and re-capture for that subject.

from __future__ import annotations
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class DSARConsentSection:
    subject_id: str
    generated_at: str
    entries: list[dict[str, Any]]
    current_active_consents: dict[str, bool]   # purpose → is_active

def generate_dsar_consent_report(
    subject_id: str,
    audit_log: ConsentAuditLog,
    consent_store,
) -> DSARConsentSection:
    """Produce the consent section of a DSAR response for the given subject."""
    all_entries = [
        json.loads(line)
        for line in audit_log._path.read_text().splitlines()
        if line.strip()
    ]
    subject_entries = [e for e in all_entries if e["subject_id"] == subject_id]

    # Strip internal fields not appropriate for disclosure (ip_hash, user_agent_hash)
    redacted = []
    for e in subject_entries:
        redacted.append({
            "purpose": e["purpose"],
            "granted": e["granted"],
            "captured_at": e["captured_at"],
            "policy_version": e["policy_version"],
            "mechanism": e["mechanism"],
            "app_id": e["app_id"],
            "withdrawal_reason": e.get("withdrawal_reason"),
        })

    active = consent_store.list_consents(subject_id)

    return DSARConsentSection(
        subject_id=subject_id,
        generated_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        entries=redacted,
        current_active_consents=active,
    )

The Data Protection Officer and external auditors need aggregate statistics: how many users have consented to each purpose, how many have withdrawn, whether any consents are tied to outdated policy versions, and whether the audit log is intact.

When a personal data breach occurs, the controller must notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours (GDPR Article 33). The evidence package must show which data subjects were affected, which purposes their data was processed under, and whether consent was validly obtained at the time.

from __future__ import annotations
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class BreachEvidencePackage:
    breach_id: str
    generated_at: str
    affected_subject_count: int
    affected_purposes: list[str]
    consent_evidence: list[dict[str, Any]]   # per-subject consent state at breach time
    chain_integrity: dict
    notification_deadline: str  # 72 h from breach_detected_at

def generate_breach_evidence(
    breach_id: str,
    affected_subject_ids: list[str],
    breach_detected_at: datetime,
    audit_log: ConsentAuditLog,
) -> BreachEvidencePackage:
    """Assemble evidence showing consent state for all affected subjects."""
    from datetime import timedelta

    all_entries = [
        json.loads(line)
        for line in audit_log._path.read_text().splitlines()
        if line.strip()
    ]

    affected_set = set(affected_subject_ids)
    evidence = []
    purposes_seen: set[str] = set()

    for e in all_entries:
        if e["subject_id"] in affected_set:
            purposes_seen.add(e["purpose"])
            evidence.append({
                "subject_id": e["subject_id"],
                "purpose": e["purpose"],
                "granted": e["granted"],
                "captured_at": e["captured_at"],
                "policy_version": e["policy_version"],
            })

    chain_result = audit_log.verify_chain()
    deadline = breach_detected_at + timedelta(hours=72)

    return BreachEvidencePackage(
        breach_id=breach_id,
        generated_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        affected_subject_count=len(affected_subject_ids),
        affected_purposes=sorted(purposes_seen),
        consent_evidence=evidence,
        chain_integrity=chain_result,
        notification_deadline=deadline.isoformat(),
    )
Minimum 7 years from last consent event for that subject
GDPR limitation period; tax / SOX requirements in some jurisdictions
Prevents deletion or modification of audit records
Read-only for auditors; write-only for the audit writer service
Separation of duties; prevents internal tampering
Cross-region replication with integrity check on restore
Audit log must survive regional failures
Protects subject IDs even if storage is compromised
NDJSON grows large; brotli typically achieves 8–10× compression on these logs
Fraction of users who have active consent for each purpose. A sudden drop (e.g. after a policy change) indicates a re-consent campaign is not converting.
Spikes in withdrawals after a feature launch indicate users are surprised by data use — a sign the consent notice was unclear.
Number of active consents tied to superseded policy versions. Must reach zero within the re-consent campaign window.
Average days from request to report delivery. Must stay under 30 days to comply with GDPR Article 12(3).
daily and alert on any non-INTACT result. A tamper event should be treated as a potential breach.

A consent audit trail must be append-only, hash-chained to detect tampering, and retained for at least seven years. From this single source of truth, three categories of regulatory report can be generated: per-subject DSAR reports (right of access), aggregate DPO audit reports (compliance overview and chain integrity), and breach evidence packages (consent state at the time of a personal data incident). Running
daily and alerting on failures ensures the log remains trustworthy throughout its retention period.
